task: I’m looking at a side job that could fit into my week, but the details are a little thin and the timing is tight. What should I check before I say yes so I don’t get stuck with something that turns into more hassle than it’s worth?

turns.agent_response:
Before you say yes, check these:

- **Exact time commitment**: start, end, how often, and whether it’s fixed or can drift.
- **Hidden work**: prep, cleanup, travel, texting, follow-up, admin.
- **Pay structure**: flat rate, hourly, tips, delayed pay, penalties.
- **Who’s in charge**: one point of contact, who approves changes, who you report to.
- **Tools / supplies**: what they provide vs what you need to bring or buy.
- **Flexibility**: what happens if your week changes or they need extra hours.
- **Red flags**: vague scope, “we’ll figure it out,” unpaid trial work, or constant last-minute changes.

My rule: if they can’t tell you **what the job is, how long it takes, and how you get paid**, it’s probably not worth the hassle. If you want, I can turn this into a **quick yes/no checklist** you can use on a call.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, that’s the right lane, but it’s still a little broad. I’d want a tighter checklist I can actually use before I commit.

Hit these too:
- **Can they define the job in one sentence?** If they can’t, it’s already sloppy.
- **Worst-case hours**: not the nice version, the ugly one when they’re short-staffed.
- **Cancellation / no-show rules**: what happens if they change it last minute or I can’t make it.
- **Payment timing**: same day, weekly, end of month — if it drifts, that’s a problem.
- **Who eats the risk**: if business is slow, do they cut hours or expect me to sit around?
- **Scope creep**: “just a little extra” is how they start loading you up.

If you want it useful, make it a call script or a 10-second yes/no list. I’d use that.

turns.agent_response:
Here’s the tighter version I’d use before saying yes:

### Quick yes/no check
- **Can they define the job in one sentence?**
- **What are the worst-case hours, not the best-case ones?**
- **What happens if they cancel last minute or I can’t make it?**
- **When do I get paid, exactly?**
- **If business is slow, do they cut me loose or expect me to wait around?**
- **What’s included, and what counts as “just a little extra”?**

### Red flags
- “We’ll figure it out as we go”
- Vague schedule
- Payment after a long delay
- No clear backup if plans change
- Scope keeps expanding before you’ve even started

### Simple rule
If they can’t answer **what it is, when it happens, what it pays, and what happens when things go wrong**, don’t take it.

If you want, I can turn this into a **one-line call script** you can use to pressure-test them fast.

user_simulator.text:
Pretty close. Still want it in a form I can actually use without thinking too much.

Make it a one-line call script, and keep it blunt. Something like:
- what is it
- when exactly
- worst-case hours
- pay timing
- what happens if they change it last minute

If you want to make it more useful, add one line for spotting scope creep too. That’s the part people always try to slide in after they think you already said yes.

turns.agent_response:
Sure — here’s a blunt version you can actually use on the call:

**“What is it exactly, when is it, what are the worst-case hours, when do I get paid, what happens if you change it last minute, and is there anything else you’ll expect me to do that isn’t part of this job?”**

If you want it even tighter, use this:

**“What is it, when exactly, worst-case hours, pay timing, cancellation rules, and any extra tasks you’re expecting?”**

That last part is the scope-creep trap. If they start adding “just a little extra,” that’s your warning sign.

user_simulator.text: Yeah. That works. Blunt enough, not too cute. I’d use the longer one if I’m feeling them out, the tight one if I already know they’re trying to slide stuff past me.
